title: Law of Mass Action, Equilibrium Constant (Kc and Kp) and its Application

concepts.title: Dynamic Equilibrium and Types of equilibrium
concepts.conceptBody:
Chemical Equilibrium

Reversible Reactions and Dynamic Equilibrium

A reaction is said to be reversible if the composition of the reaction mixture on the approach of equilibrium at a given temperature is the same irrespective of the initial state of the system, i.e., irrespective of the fact whether we start with the reactants or the products. Some examples of reversible reaction are listed below:

$\text{A}+\text{B}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}+\text{D}$

${{\text{H}}_{2}}(~\text{g})+{{\text{I}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\text{ }\rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}(\text{g})$

\[3\text{Fe}(\text{s})+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{g})\text{ }\rightleftharpoons \text{F}{{\text{e}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}(~\text{s})+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\]

$\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}}(\text{aq})+\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}(\text{aq})\rightleftharpoons \text{Fe}3+(\text{aq})+\text{Ag}(\text{s})$

It is an experimental fact that most of the processes including chemical reactions, when carried out in a closed vessel, do not go to completion. Under these conditions, a process starts by itself or by initiation, continues for some time at diminishing rate and ultimately appears to stop. The reactants may still be present but they do not appear to change into products any more.

Characteristics of Chemical Equilibrium:

  • Chemical equilibrium, at a given temperature, is characterised by constancy of certain observable properties such as pressure, conc., density.
  • Chemical equilibrium can be approached from either side.
  • Chemical equilibrium is dynamic in nature.
  • A catalyst does not alter the position of equilibrium. It accelerates both the forward and reverse reactions.

Types of Equilibria

There are mainly two types of equilibria

(a) Homogeneous: Equilibrium is said to be homogeneous if reactants and products are in same phase.

${{\text{H}}_{2}}(~\text{g})+{{\text{I}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\text{ }\rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}(\text{g})$

${{\text{N}}_{2}}(~\text{g})+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\text{ }\rightleftharpoons 2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}(~\text{g})$

${{\text{N}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}(~\text{g})\text{ }\rightleftharpoons 2\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}(\text{l})+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{OH}(\text{l})\text{ }\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COO}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}(\text{l})+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})$

(b) Heterogeneous: Equilibrium is said to be heterogeneous if reactants and products are in different phases.

$\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(~\text{s})\text{ }\rightleftharpoons \text{CaO}(\text{s})+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{HS}(\text{s})\text{ }\rightleftharpoons \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}(~\text{g})+{{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}(~\text{g})$

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}(~\text{s})\rightleftharpoons 2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}(~\text{g})+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$

concepts.title: Rate of Reaction [definition]
concepts.conceptBody:
Rate of a Reaction

The rate of a reaction is defined as the decrease in concentration per unit time of the reactants or the increase in concentration per unit time of the products.

For example, consider

$\text{A}+2~\text{B}\to 3\text{C}$

In the atomic reaction, according to the stoichiometric coefficients, 2 moles of $\text{B}$ disappear for every mole of $\text{A}$ reacting and 3 moles of $\text{C}$ are formed for every mole of $\text{A}$ disappears.

$\therefore $ $-\frac{\text{d}[\text{A}]}{\text{dt}}=\frac{-1}{2}\frac{\text{d}[\text{B}]}{\text{dt}}=\frac{1}{3}\frac{\text{d}[\text{C}]}{\text{dt}}=\text{ rate of reaction }$

Here –ve sign is put before [A] and [B] because their molar concentration decrease with time.

concepts.title: Factors affecting rate of reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Factors Influencing the Rate of a Reaction

(i) Concentration of the reactant species: Greater the concentrations of the reactants, the greater is the rate.

(ii) Temperature: The rate of a reaction increases with increase in temperature. In general for most reactions the rate constant doubles with every 10 rise in temperature.

(iii) Nature of the reactants: Reactions which do not involve considerable bond rearrangement are generally faster at room temperature than those which involve considerable bond rearrangements.

(iv) Effect of catalyst: A reaction proceeds much faster in the presence of a catalyst. For instance, the hydrolysis of an ester is very fast when catalyzed by ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions.

(v) Effect of radiation: Photochemical reactions are faster than thermal ones because in the former all the energy of the photons is completely used in exciting the molecules while in the latter, the energy is distributed at random translational, rotational and vibrational modes of motion.

concepts.title: Law of mass action and Equilibrium constant
concepts.conceptBody:
Law of Mass Action and Equilibrium Constant

The rate at which a substance reacts is proportional to its active mass and the rate of a chemical reaction is proportional to the product of the active masses of the reacting substance. 

The reactions are generally reversible, that is, they can proceed both ways. A reaction is said to have attained equilibrium when the rate of the forward reaction equals that of the backward reaction.

Let us consider a general case of a reversible reaction,

$\text{aA}+\text{bB}\rightleftharpoons \text{mM}+\text{nN}$

Applying the law of mass action:

Rate of the forward reaction $\propto {{[\text{A}]}^{\text{a}}}{{[\text{B}]}^{\text{b}}}$

or Rate of the forward reaction $={{\text{k}}_{1}}{{[~\text{A}]}^{\text{a}}}{{[\text{B}]}^{\text{b}}}$

Rate of backward reaction $\propto {{[\text{M}]}^{\text{m}}}{{[\text{N}]}^{\text{n}}}$

or Rate of backward reaction $={{\text{k}}_{2}}{{[\text{M}]}^{\text{m}}}{{[\text{N}]}^{\text{n}}}$

At equilibrium:

Rate of forward reaction = rate of backward reaction

${{\text{k}}_{1}}{{[~\text{A}]}^{\text{a}}}{{[\text{B}]}^{\text{b}}}={{\text{k}}_{2}}{{[\text{M}]}^{\text{m}}}{{[\text{N}]}^{\text{n}}}$

$\frac{{{\text{k}}_{1}}}{{{\text{k}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{[\text{M}]}^{\text{m}}}{{[\text{N}]}^{\text{n}}}}{{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{a}}}{{[\text{B}]}^{\text{b}}}}$

or ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}=\frac{{{\text{k}}_{1}}}{{{\text{k}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{[\text{M}]}^{\text{m}}}{{[\text{N}]}^{\text{n}}}}{{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{a}}}{{[\text{B}]}^{\text{b}}}}$ … (1)

[ ] represents concentration in moles per litre. Here, all concentrations are at equilibrium. ${{\text{k}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{k}}_{2}}$ are known as the rate constants of the forward and backward reactions respectively.

Chemical equilibrium is dynamic in the sense that individual molecules are continually reacting, even though the overall composition of the reaction mixture does not change. The other criteria of a chemical equilibrium are the same equilibrium state can be attained from both sides of the reaction and a reaction which attains equilibrium is always incomplete.

The rate constant of any reaction is defined as the rate of the reaction when the concentration of each reactant is unity. The rate constant of a reaction depends on the temperature and the catalyst.

${{\text{k}}_{\text{c}}}$, the ratio of the rate constants, is known as the equilibrium constant. Now, if the above reaction is supposed to be a homogeneous gaseous one, the equilibrium constant may also represented as

${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}=\frac{\text{p}_{\text{M}}^{\text{m}}\cdot \text{p}_{\text{N}}^{\text{n}}}{\text{p}_{\text{A}}^{\text{a}}\cdot \text{p}_{\text{B}}^{\text{b}}}$ … (2)

where ${{\text{p}}^{\prime }}\text{s}*$ represent the partial pressures at equilibrium.

concepts.title: Relation between Kp and Kc
concepts.conceptBody:
Reaction between ${{\mathbf{K}}_{\mathbf{p}}}\mathbf{ \And }\,\,{{\mathbf{K}}_{\mathbf{c}}}$

The equilibrium constant ${{K}_{p}}$ and ${{K}_{c}}$ is known as pressure equilibrium constant. The magnitude of ${{K}_{p}}$ and ${{K}_{c}}$ is a measure of the extent to which the reaction occurs. The equilibrium constants depend only on temperature. For a given reaction ${{K}_{p}}$ and ${{K}_{c}}$ may be equal and may also be different depending on the stoichiometry of the reaction. ${{K}_{p}}$ and ${{K}_{c}}$ and are related as

\(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}(\mathrm{R T})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}}\) … (3)

where, $\Delta \text{n}=$ no. of moles of the gaseous product - no. of moles of the gaseous reactants (in the balanced equation and not in the reaction)

or $\Delta \text{n}=(\text{m}+\text{n})-(\text{a}+\text{b})$

But both the equilibrium constants for a given reaction give the same information about the state of equilibrium when the reaction at equilibrium is subjected to a change in temperature, pressure or concentration as also expressed by Le Chatelier's principle. The units of ${{K}_{p}}$ and ${{K}_{c}}$ are not fixed and depend on the stoichiometry of the reaction. In case the number of moles of the reactant and that of the product are same, ${{K}_{p}}$ and ${{K}_{c}}$ do not have any unit.

[ Note: There is a third type of equilibrium constant, much less in use, called ${{K}_{x}}$, when concentrations are expressed in terms of mole fraction (x).]

Thus, ${{K}_{x}}=\frac{x_{M}^{m}\cdot x_{N}^{n}}{x_{A}^{a}\cdot x_{B}^{b}}$

Relating this expression with equations 1, 2 and 3, we get

${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}\text{ }={{\text{K}}_{\text{x}}}\cdot {{\text{p}}^{\Delta \text{n}}}={{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}{{(\text{RT})}^{\Delta \text{n}}}$

for $\Delta \text{n }=0:{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}={{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}={{\text{K}}_{\text{x}}}$

${{K}_{x}}$ does not have any unit. The mole fraction equilibrium constant ${{K}_{x}}$, unlike ${{K}_{p}}$ and ${{K}_{c}}$, may depend on pressure and volume.

Important Relationship Involving Equilibrium Constant:

(a) If you reverse an equation ${{K}_{C}}\text{ or }{{K}_{P}}$ is inverted i.e.

If $\text{A}+\text{B}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}+\text{D}$ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}=10$

then $\text{C}+\text{D}\rightleftharpoons \text{A}+\text{B}$ $\text{K}_{\text{c}}^{\prime }={{10}^{-1}}$

(b) If you multiply each of the coefficient in a balanced equation by factor m, then equilibrium constant is raised to the same factor

If $\frac{1}{2}~{{\text{N}}_{2}}+\frac{1}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\rightleftharpoons \text{NO},$ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}=5$

then for ${{\text{N}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}$ ${{\text{K}}^{\prime }}\text{c}=\text{K}_{\text{c}}^{2}={{5}^{2}}=25$

(c) If you divide each of the coefficients in a balanced equation by the factor $m$, then new equilibrium constant is mth root of the previous value.

If $2\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\rightleftharpoons 2\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}\quad =25$

then for $\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+\frac{1}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\rightleftharpoons \text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ $~\text{K}_{\text{c}}^{\prime }={{\left( {{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{2}}}=\sqrt{25}=5$

(d) When you combine (i.e., add) individual equation, multiply their equilibrium constants to obtain the equilibrium constant for the reaction.

If${{\text{K}}_{1}},~{{\text{K}}_{2}},~{{\text{K}}_{3}}$ are stepwise equilibrium constant for

$\text{A}\rightleftharpoons \text{B}$ …(i)

$\text{B}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}$ …(ii)

$\text{C}\rightleftharpoons \text{D}$ …(iii)

then for $\text{A}\rightleftharpoons \text{D}$ [obtained by adding (i), (ii) and (iii) net equilibrium constant is,

$\text{K}={{\text{K}}_{1}}~{{\text{K}}_{2}}~{{\text{K}}_{3}}$

(e) Do not include concentration terms for pure solids and pure liquids in equilibrium constant expression.

for $\text{C}(\text{s})+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{g})\text{ }\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})+{{\text{H}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}\text{ }=\frac{[\text{CO}]\left[ {{\text{H}}_{2}} \right]}{\left[ {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right]}$

for $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(~\text{s})\text{ }\rightleftharpoons \text{CaO}(\text{s})+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}\text{ }={{\text{P}}_{\text{CO}2}}\text{ and }{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}={{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}(\text{RT})$

concepts.title: Interpretation of Equilibrium constant
concepts.conceptBody:
Interpretation of Equilibrium Constant

The equilibrium constant for a reaction tells about the tendency of a reaction to proceed to products.

(i) If the concentration of each reactant and product in a general reaction

$\text{aA}+\text{bB}\rightleftharpoons \text{mM}+\text{nN}$

are such that,

$\frac{{{[\text{M}]}^{\text{m}}}\cdot {{[\text{N}]}^{\text{n}}}}{{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{a}}}\cdot {{[\text{B}]}^{\text{b}}}}=\text{K}$ (equilibrium constant)

the reaction is at equilibrium.

(ii) If the concentration are such that,

$\frac{{{[\text{M}]}^{\text{m}}}\cdot {{[\text{N}]}^{\text{n}}}}{{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{a}}}\cdot {{[\text{B}]}^{\text{b}}}}<\text{K}$

the reaction will proceed from left to right in order to attain equilibrium, or in other words, the concentration factor or the reaction quotient increases and becomes equal to $\text{K}$.

(iii) And, if the concentrations are such that,

$\frac{{{[\text{M}]}^{\text{m}}}\cdot {{[\text{N}]}^{\text{n}}}}{{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{a}}}\cdot {{[\text{B}]}^{\text{b}}}}>\text{K}$

the reaction will proceed from right to left in order to attain equilibrium, or in other words, the concentration factor or the reaction quotient decreases and becomes equal to $\text{K}$.

If the concentration factor, i.e.,

is expressed as reaction quotient, $Q$, we have,

1. $\text{Q}>\text{K}$: Reaction proceeds in backward direction until equilibrium is established.

2. $\text{Q}=\text{K}:$ Reaction is at equilibrium.

3. $\text{Q}<\text{K}$: Reaction proceeds in forward direction until equilibrium is established.

Significance of the Magnitude of Equilibrium Constant

(i) A very large value of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ or ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ signifies that the forward reaction goes to completion or very nearly so.

(ii) A very small value of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ or ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ signifies that the forward reaction does not occur to any significant extent.

(iii) A reaction is most likely to reach a state of equilibrium in which both reactants in which both reactants and products are present if the numerical value of ${{K}_{c}}$ or ${{K}_{p}}$ is neither very large nor very small.

concepts.title: Formation of HI
concepts.conceptBody:
Formation of HI

$\begin{matrix} a & {} & b & {} & 0 & \text{Initial moles } \\ {{H}_{2}}(g) & + & {{I}_{2}}(g) & \rightleftharpoons & 2HI(g) & {} \\ (a-x) & {} & (b-x) & {} & 2x & \text{Moles at equilibrium } \\ \frac{(a-x)}{V} & {} & \frac{(b-x)}{V} & {} & \frac{2x}{V} & \text{Molar concentration at eqb}\text{.} \\ \end{matrix}$

where $\text{x}$ is the number of moles of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ or ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$ converted to $\text{HI}$ at equilibrium and $\text{V}$ is the volume in litres of the container.

${{K}_{c}}\text{ }=\frac{{{[HI]}^{2}}}{\left[ {{H}_{2}} \right]\left[ {{I}_{2}} \right]}=\frac{{{(2x/V)}^{2}}}{\frac{(a-x)}{V}\cdot \frac{(b-x)}{V}}$

$=\frac{4{{x}^{2}}}{(a-x)(b-x)}$

Further, total number of moles at equilibrium

$=(a-x)+(b-x)+2x$

$=a+b$

Let the total pressure be $p$.

${{\text{p}}_{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}=\frac{(\text{a}-\text{x})}{(\text{a}+\text{b})}\cdot \text{p};{{\text{p}}_{{{\text{I}}_{2}}}}=\frac{(\text{b}-\text{x})}{(\text{a}+\text{b})}\cdot \text{p and }$

${{\text{p}}_{\text{HI}}}=\frac{2\text{x}}{(\text{a}+\text{b})}\cdot \text{p}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}=\frac{\text{p}_{\text{HI}}^{2}}{{{\text{p}}_{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}{{\text{p}}_{{{\text{I}}_{2}}}}}=\frac{4{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{(\text{a}-\text{x})(\text{b}-\text{x})}$$\text{(substituting the value of p }\!\!'\!\!\text{ s})$

concepts.title: Formation of NH3
concepts.conceptBody:
Formation of $\mathbf{N}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$

$\begin{matrix} a & {} & b & {} & 0 & \text{Initial moles } \\ {{N}_{2}}(g) & + & 3{{H}_{2}}(g) & \rightleftharpoons & 2N{{H}_{3}}(g) & {} \\ (a-x) & {} & (b-3x) & {} & 2x & \text{Moles at equilibrium } \\ \frac{(a-x)}{V} & {} & \frac{(b-x)}{V} & {} & \frac{2x}{V} & \text{Molar concentration at equilibrium} \\ \end{matrix}$

where x is the number of moles of ${{\text{N}}_{2}}$ which converted to $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ at equilibrium.

$\therefore \quad {{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}=\frac{{{\left[ \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right]}^{2}}}{\left[ ~{{\text{N}}_{2}} \right]{{\left[ {{\text{H}}_{2}} \right]}^{3}}}=\frac{{{\left( \frac{2\text{x}}{\text{V}} \right)}^{2}}}{\left( \frac{\text{a}-\text{x}}{\text{V}} \right){{\left( \frac{\text{b}-3\text{x}}{\text{V}} \right)}^{3}}}$

Further, total number of moles at equilibrium

$=\text{a}-\text{x}+\text{b}-3\text{x}+2\text{x}=(\text{a}+\text{b}-2\text{x})$

${{K}_{p}}=\frac{P_{NH}^{2}}{{{P}_{{{N}_{2}}}}\cdot {{P}_{{{H}_{2}}}}}=\frac{{{\left\{ \left( \frac{2x}{a+b-2x} \right)\cdot p \right\}}^{2}}}{\left\{ \frac{a-x}{a+b-2x}\cdot p \right\}{{\left\{ \frac{b-3x}{a+b-2x}\cdot p \right\}}^{3}}}$

concepts.title: Dissociation of PCl5
concepts.conceptBody:
Dissociation of $\mathbf{PC}{{\mathbf{l}}_{\mathbf{5}}}$

$\begin{matrix} a & {} & 0 & {} & 0 & \text{Initial moles } \\ PC{{l}_{5}}(g) & \rightleftharpoons & PC{{l}_{3}}(g) & + & C{{l}_{2}}(g) & {} \\ (a-x) & {} & x & {} & x & \text{Moles at equilibrium } \\ \frac{(a-x)}{V} & {} & \frac{x}{V} & {} & \frac{x}{V} & \text{Molar concentration at equilibrium} \\ \end{matrix}$

Here, $\text{x}$ is the number of moles of $\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{5}}$ converted to the products at equilibrium. But if $\text{x}$ represents degree of dissociation, i.e., fraction of the total number of molecules undergoing dissociation, the equilibrium moles and equilibrium molar concentration of each species will be as follows:

$\begin{matrix} a & {} & 0 & {} & 0 & \text{Initial moles } \\ PC{{l}_{5}}(g) & \rightleftharpoons & PC{{l}_{3}}(g) & + & C{{l}_{2}}(g) & {} \\ a(1-x) & {} & ax & {} & ax & \text{Moles at equilibrium } \\ \frac{a(1-x)}{V} & {} & \frac{ax}{V} & {} & \frac{ax}{V} & \text{Molar concentration at equilibrium} \\ \end{matrix}$

From the values of given above, equations for ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$ and ${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}$ can be derived.

concepts.title: Dissociation of halogen molecules
concepts.conceptBody:
Dissociation of Halogen Molecules

a 0 Initial moles

${{\text{X}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\rightleftharpoons 2\text{X}(\text{g})$ {If x is the number of moles of ${{X}_{2}}$ converted to X at eqb.}

(a – x) 2x Moles at equilibrium

$\left( \frac{a-x}{V} \right)$ $\left( \frac{2x}{V} \right)$ Molar concentration at equilibrium

But if $\text{x}$ is the degree of dissociation:

${{\text{X}}_{\text{2}}}(\text{g})\rightleftharpoons 2\text{X}(\text{g})$

$\begin{matrix} a(1-x) & 2ax & \text{ Moles at equilibrium}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{ } \\ \left( \frac{a(1-x)}{V} \right) & \left( \frac{2ax}{V} \right) & \text{ Molar concentration at eqb}\text{. } \\ \end{matrix}$

Derive ${{K}_{p}}$ and ${{K}_{c}}$.

concepts.title: Equations for Equilibrium constant
concepts.conceptBody:
Homogeneous Equilibria and Equations for Equilibrium Constant (Equilibrium Pressure is $P$ atm in a V L Flask)

Heterogeneouos Equilibria and Equation for Equilibrium Constant (Equilibrium Pressure is $\mathbf{P}$ atm)

concepts.title: Relation between Vapour density and Degree of Dissociation
concepts.conceptBody:
Relation Between Vapour Density and Degree of Dissociation

Let equilibrium reaction be with $\text{x}$ as degree of dissociation

$\text{A}\rightleftharpoons \text{yB}$

given by $x=\frac{D-d}{(y-1)d}$

where $\text{D}$ is the vapour density of A before dissociation, $\text{d}$ the vapour density of the reaction mixture consisting of $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$, and $\text{y}$ the no. of products.

D $\quad 2=$ mol. wt of A

d $\quad 2=$ mol. wt of mixture (also called abnormal mol. wt. of A)

Thus $\text{x}=\frac{\text{D}-\text{d}}{\text{d}}\text{ for }\left( \text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{5}}\rightleftharpoons \text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right)\text{ with y}=2$
